retrieved_docs.title: Vertebrobasilar Stroke -- Treatment / Management -- Ischemic Strokes
retrieved_docs.content: After treatment with TPA and/or thrombectomy, the patient is later observed in a neuro focused ICU setting for close monitoring of vitals. Blood pressure is permissively elevated to 180/90 mmHg to allow tissue reperfusion in the first 24 hours after the intervention. If no therapy is given, blood pressure is more permissive to 220/110 mmHg. Strict blood pressure can be controlled using IV antihypertensives such as nicardipine, clevidipine, hydralazine, or labetalol. Hourly neuro checks are also performed to assess for a change in their neurological exam to indicate an acute complication such as intracranial hemorrhage, edema with midline shift or herniation, or a repeat ischemic stroke. If a change is documented, a stat CT of the head without contrast should be obtained.

retrieved_docs.title: Ischemic Stroke -- Pearls and Other Issues
retrieved_docs.content: An antiplatelet agent should be initiated within 24 hours of patient presentation.

retrieved_docs.title: Ventral Cord Syndrome -- Pertinent Studies and Ongoing Trials
retrieved_docs.content: Many of the current treatments for spinal cord infarcts show only modest improvements in the functional outcome if used as monotherapy, which is probably attributable to the fact that many factors lead to the development of permanent neurological deficits. Thus, future treatment trials should consider a combined and multimodal treatment approach. [9]

retrieved_docs.title: Alteplase -- Administration -- Clinical Evidence
retrieved_docs.content: The NINDS trial was a randomized trial of IV alteplase versus placebo in patients with acute ischemic stroke treated within 3 hours of symptom onset. Alteplase improved functional outcomes at three months in comparison to placebo. [5] [6] [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Cerebral Ischemia(Archived) -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: The management of global brain ischemia is generally targeted at correcting the underlying cause. Supportive care should be undertaken to ensure adequate cerebral blood flow; this may be facilitated by placing the patient flat or in the Trendelenburg position while other supportive measures are being taken.

retrieved_docs.title: Ischemic Stroke -- Treatment / Management -- Cerebellar or cerebral edema
retrieved_docs.content: Cerebral edema following acute ischemic stroke initially results from cytotoxic edema due to cellular swelling, followed by vasogenic edema as the blood-brain barrier becomes compromised. The extent of edema generally correlates with the size of the infarct and is typically not clinically significant in lacunar strokes. However, in larger strokes, edema can become symptomatic, leading to worsening neurological deficits and impaired consciousness due to herniation. Cerebral edema typically peaks between 3 and 5 days after the onset of ischemic stroke. [59]

retrieved_docs.content: The failure of the aforementioned substances in clinical trials versus their apparent successes in animal studies raises a fundamental question about the validity of animal ischemia models and their ability to simulate human cerebral ischemia accurately. However, ongoing research in targeted treatments and advancements in modern, functional, and temporal neuroimaging offer promising avenues for future promise.

retrieved_docs.title: Febuxostat -- Adverse Effects
retrieved_docs.content: Nervous system: Altered taste, balance disorder, cerebrovascular accident, Guillain-Barre syndrome, headache, hemiparesis, hypoesthesia, hyposmia, lacunar infarction, lethargy, mental impairment, migraine, paresthesia, somnolence, transient ischemic attack, tremor

retrieved_docs.title: Basilar Artery Infarct -- Pertinent Studies and Ongoing Trials -- Heparin after an Acute Ischemic Stroke
retrieved_docs.content: TOAST (Trial of ORG 10172 in Acute Stroke Treatment) trial [1998] showed no favorable 3-months outcomes with the use of heparinoids after an acute ischemic stroke. [39]

retrieved_docs.title: Tenecteplase -- Administration -- Acute Ischemic Stroke
retrieved_docs.content: From all the available data about tenecteplase in acute ischemic stroke, one can conclude that no clear positive phase 3 trial shows its superiority over alteplase. 0.4 mg/kg of tenecteplase showed no advantage over the 0.25 mg/kg dose. Given its longer half-life so that it can be given as a single bolus dose and its lower cost, tenecteplase is becoming more accepted in most stroke centers in the USA over alteplase.

retrieved_docs.title: Stroke Center Certification -- Introduction -- Thrombolytic Therapy in Acute Ischemic Stroke Management
retrieved_docs.content: Recent studies have examined the standard dose of alteplase, which involves administering 0.9 mg/kg alteplase IV, with a maximum total dose of 90 mg. This dose is given by administering 10% of the total dose as an initial IV bolus over 1 minute, followed by infusing the remainder over 60 minutes. These studies have compared this standard regimen with a rapid infusion of tenecteplase at 0.25 mg/kg. Multiple studies have shown that tenecteplase at both 0.1 and 0.25 mg/kg did not exhibit superiority over alteplase regarding neurological outcomes in patients with AIS within the 3- to 4.5-hour window. However, for patients with LVO, the results did not reach statistical significance. [4] [5] [6] [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Tenecteplase -- Administration -- Acute Ischemic Stroke
retrieved_docs.content: The most recently published AcT trial in Canada is a large randomized open-label trial comparing 0.25 mg/kg of tenecteplase and alteplase. This trial demonstrated the non-inferiority of tenecteplase versus alteplase. [19]

retrieved_docs.title: Alteplase -- Administration -- Clinical Evidence
retrieved_docs.content: In the ECASS trial, alteplase administered between 3 and 4.5 hours after onset of acute ischemic stroke symptoms resulted in a lower incidence of the primary end-point, disability at three months, compared to placebo. However, alteplase was more frequently associated with symptomatic intracranial hemorrhage than placebo.

retrieved_docs.title: Stroke Center Certification -- Introduction -- Thrombolytic Therapy in Acute Ischemic Stroke Management
retrieved_docs.content: In terms of safety, research findings have been somewhat varied, with some studies indicating a tendency toward fewer intracranial bleeding complications with low-dose tenecteplase (0.1 or 0.25 mg/kg) compared to high-dose tenecteplase (0.4 mg/kg) and standard-dose alteplase. A larger randomized controlled trial with a higher statistical power could provide valuable insights into the efficacy and safety profiles of tenecteplase compared to alteplase in LVO patients eligible for embolectomy.

retrieved_docs.content: Transient cerebral ischemia is characterized by a transitory clinical course, indicating that the neurological deficits associated with this brief interruption of cerebral blood flow are temporary and typically resolve quickly without permanent damage.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 2 -- Community 749
retrieved_docs.content: Asymptomatic occlusion of the basilar artery is understood through the lens of general wellbeing, and general wellbeing itself is interpreted by the condition described as غشي (syncope). The term Transitory denotes a clinical course that is characterized by غشي, and this transitory course is the clinical pattern of transient cerebral ischemia. Transient cerebral ischemia is closely linked to نوبة إقفارية عابرة (transient ischemic attack), reflecting their shared pathophysiology. Atrial fibrillation (الرجفان الأذيني) is clinically associated with rheumatic heart disease (مرض قلبي روماتزمي) and also with transient ischemic attacks, underscoring its role in cerebrovascular risk. Consequently, the hormonal agents drospirenone and estetrol monohydrate are contraindicated in patients with atrial fibrillation, highlighting the need for careful medication selection in this population.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 2 -- Community 891
retrieved_docs.content: Heparin, an anticoagulant medication, may be employed in the management of احتشاء دماغي (cerebral infarction or stroke). By inhibiting clot formation, heparin can help prevent the propagation of thrombi in patients experiencing this type of brain injury, underscoring its therapeutic role in acute stroke care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 30 -- Level 1 -- Community 248
retrieved_docs.content: distinguishing non‑infarcting stenotic disease. The clinical course descriptor “Transitory” is applied to transient cerebral ischemia, which corresponds to a transient ischemic attack (نوبة إقفارية عابرة), highlighting the temporary nature of some ischemic events. Overall, occlusion or stenosis of precerebral arteries is a pivotal pathological mechanism that can lead to multiple forms of cerebral infarction, may be asymptomatic, is associated with intracranial arterial sclerosis, and is integrated within a structured classification of cerebrovascular circulatory diseases. These relationships inform diagnosis, risk assessment, therapeutic decision‑making (including endarterectomy), and the broader understanding of disease hierarchy in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 2452
retrieved_docs.content: Transient cerebral ischemia, referred to in Arabic as نوبة إقفارية عابرة, denotes a brief, reversible episode of reduced blood flow to a focal area of the brain that does not result in permanent tissue damage. This condition is characterized by temporary neurological deficits that resolve within minutes to hours and serves as an important clinical warning sign for the potential development of a full‑blown stroke. Recognizing the relationship between transient cerebral ischemia and نوبة إقفارية عابرة is essential for prompt evaluation, risk stratification, and implementation of preventive strategies to reduce the likelihood of subsequent cerebrovascular events.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2668
retrieved_docs.content: embolie; arteria basilaris, referred to in Arabic as انصمام الشريان المخي, denotes an embolic occlusion of the basilar artery. This condition occurs when a thrombus or other embolic material blocks the basilar artery, which supplies blood to the brainstem, cerebellum, and posterior cerebral circulation. The resulting ischemia can lead to severe neurological deficits, including loss of consciousness, cranial nerve palsies, motor weakness, and potentially fatal outcomes if not promptly recognized and treated. Early diagnosis through neuroimaging and rapid reperfusion strategies are critical for improving patient prognosis and minimizing long‑term disability.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2302
retrieved_docs.content: Migraine with ischemic complication is a broader clinical category that encompasses forms of migraine in which vascular ischemia occurs, and persistent migraine aura with cerebral infarction is a specific manifestation within this category. Persistent migraine aura with cerebral infarction is also classified as an acute cerebrovascular disease, reflecting its nature as a sudden, stroke‑like event caused by interruption of cerebral blood flow during a prolonged migraine aura. By linking migraine with ischemic complication to acute cerebrovascular disease through the specific entity of persistent migraine aura with cerebral infarction, the knowledge underscores that certain prolonged migraine auras can evolve into acute cerebral infarctions, highlighting the importance of recognizing these migraine‑related ischemic complications for timely diagnosis and appropriate stroke‑focused management.

retrieved_docs.content: surgical removal of atherosclerotic plaque—endarterectomy of head‑and‑neck vessels (استئصال باطنة الشريان)—is indicated in the context of arterial thrombosis or embolism. The intracranial arterial structure can reveal intracerebral atherosclerotic changes, providing an indirect site for such procedures. Together, these entities illustrate the interconnected pathophysiology of precerebral arterial disease, basilar artery involvement, cerebral infarction, transient ischemic events, clinical presentation, and both medical and surgical management strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 3 -- Community 2354
retrieved_docs.content: The arteria basilaris is identified as the anatomical location where transient cerebral ischemia occurs. Transient cerebral ischemia, also referred to by its Arabic term نوبة إقفارية عابرة, represents a brief episode of reduced cerebral blood flow that can produce temporary neurological deficits. Recognizing that transient cerebral ischemia originates in the basilar artery is important for clinicians, as ischemic events in this posterior circulation can lead to distinct clinical presentations and influence diagnostic and therapeutic strategies.
